course.title: HTTP Servers in Go 1.26: Secure Production APIs
course.description: Go developers who can build robust clients and relational data systems and now need production-grade origin services and HTTP APIs. This original, research-backed path uses repeated retrieval, cumulative artifacts, changed-case labs, stakeholder projects, and a performance exam.
outcomes: Demonstrate go 1.26.5 net/http service lifecycle, semantics, servemux, handlers, middleware, request and response boundaries, body limits, streaming, context, server protocols and timeouts, concurrency, postgresql-backed repositories, consistency, authentication, authorization, cors and csrf, webhooks, ssrf, reverse proxies, smuggling defense, tests, fuzzing, race detection, observability, health, openapi 3.2, compatibility, deployment, and recovery through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate runnable deterministic pure-go decision models plus explicit compile, race, listener, tls, postgresql, proxy, load, and production transfer gates through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
Demonstrate five cumulative production service projects and a performance-based certification exam through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
sharedRequirements: Every activity retrieves Go, HTTP client, SQL, accessibility, security, context, testing, and evidence habits before adding one server boundary.

Browser Go is deterministic and isolated. It models request, handler, dependency, response, and cleanup decisions but never opens a listener, contacts a network or database, executes host commands, reads host state, or handles real credentials.

Passing work requires exact request and route inputs, authority and resource limits, response and effect evidence, changed and failure cases, cleanup and recovery, and explicit full-toolchain transfer limits.

masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Use safe-method invariants, Go CrossOriginProtection, Sec-Fetch-Site and Origin policy, tokens when needed, and narrowly reviewed bypasses.

competencies.statement: Select host-only scope, __Host or __Secure prefixes, Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite, Partitioned, expiry, and rotation for the intended browser flow.
misconceptions: HttpOnly prevents a browser from sending the cookie in a cross-site request.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Select host-only scope, __Host or __Secure prefixes, Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite, Partitioned, expiry, and rotation for the intended browser flow.

competencies.statement: Set content-type protection, framing, referrer, transport, content security, permissions, and cache policy according to whether responses are API data or HTML.
misconceptions: Security headers are interchangeable boilerplate and stricter values cannot break required browser flows.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Set content-type protection, framing, referrer, transport, content security, permissions, and cache policy according to whether responses are API data or HTML.

competencies.statement: Bound and retain exact raw body bytes, select expected signature scheme and key, compare in constant time, and only then parse and authorize the event.
misconceptions: Re-encoding decoded JSON produces the same bytes and is safe for webhook signature verification.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Bound and retain exact raw body bytes, select expected signature scheme and key, compare in constant time, and only then parse and authorize the event.

competencies.statement: Validate timestamp or nonce window, event ID uniqueness, tenant, type, ordering, duplicate delivery, and durable processing state before effects.
misconceptions: A valid signature proves a webhook event is new and has not already been processed.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Validate timestamp or nonce window, event ID uniqueness, tenant, type, ordering, duplicate delivery, and durable processing state before effects.

competencies.statement: Acknowledge only after durable acceptance, move long work to bounded processing, classify retries, make effects idempotent, and expose operator recovery.
misconceptions: Returning 2xx before recording the event is safe because the provider can always retry.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Acknowledge only after durable acceptance, move long work to bounded processing, classify retries, make effects idempotent, and expose operator recovery.

competencies.statement: Treat server-side URLs, redirects, DNS results, IP ranges, schemes, ports, credentials, and response size as egress policy enforced on every hop.
misconceptions: Validating the input hostname once prevents redirects and DNS rebinding from reaching private services.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Treat server-side URLs, redirects, DNS results, IP ranges, schemes, ports, credentials, and response size as egress policy enforced on every hop.

competencies.statement: Bound costly domain actions by principal, resource, workflow state, velocity, inventory, and anomaly evidence beyond raw request rate.
misconceptions: A generic requests-per-minute limit prevents scraping, scalping, spam, and workflow automation abuse.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Bound costly domain actions by principal, resource, workflow state, velocity, inventory, and anomaly evidence beyond raw request rate.

competencies.statement: Trace downstream and upstream connections, hop-by-hop versus end-to-end fields, termination points, authority, scheme, client address, and failure ownership.
misconceptions: A reverse proxy makes one transparent end-to-end HTTP connection from client to origin.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Trace downstream and upstream connections, hop-by-hop versus end-to-end fields, termination points, authority, scheme, client address, and failure ownership.

competencies.statement: Use ReverseProxy.Rewrite with separate inbound and outbound requests, SetURL and reviewed SetXForwarded behavior, and reject deprecated Director trust assumptions.
misconceptions: Director is equivalent to Rewrite and remains safe when it adds an authentication header.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Use ReverseProxy.Rewrite with separate inbound and outbound requests, SetURL and reviewed SetXForwarded behavior, and reject deprecated Director trust assumptions.

competencies.statement: Strip untrusted forwarding fields, rebuild them only at trusted hops, constrain backend targets, remove hop-by-hop state, and prevent header spoofing.
misconceptions: Appending a trusted proxy value to client-supplied X-Forwarded-For makes the entire chain trustworthy.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Strip untrusted forwarding fields, rebuild them only at trusted hops, constrain backend targets, remove hop-by-hop state, and prevent header spoofing.

competencies.statement: Prevent request smuggling by rejecting ambiguous Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding, malformed whitespace, conflicting parsers, oversized fields, and unsafe downgrade paths per RFC 9112.
misconceptions: Because Go parses the front-end request safely, every downstream proxy and application will interpret it identically.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Prevent request smuggling by rejecting ambiguous Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding, malformed whitespace, conflicting parsers, oversized fields, and unsafe downgrade paths per RFC 9112.

competencies.statement: Handle unparsable query parameters, backend timeouts, bounded bodies, ModifyResponse, ErrorHandler, streaming, Alt-Svc, and sanitized failure evidence.
misconceptions: Copying RawQuery always preserves intent and cannot create a parser differential with the backend.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Handle unparsable query parameters, backend timeouts, bounded bodies, ModifyResponse, ErrorHandler, streaming, Alt-Svc, and sanitized failure evidence.

competencies.statement: Use httptest.NewRequestWithContext, NewRecorder, Result, headers, body limits, route values, and table cases while naming recorder limitations.
misconceptions: ResponseRecorder.Code always contains the implicit 200 even when a handler writes nothing.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Use httptest.NewRequestWithContext, NewRecorder, Result, headers, body limits, route values, and table cases while naming recorder limitations.

competencies.statement: Use NewServer or NewTLSServer for routing, middleware, redirects, cookies, protocol, streaming, and client-server integration with deterministic cleanup.
misconceptions: A ResponseRecorder test proves listener, TLS, connection, flush, and full middleware behavior.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Use NewServer or NewTLSServer for routing, middleware, redirects, cookies, protocol, streaming, and client-server integration with deterministic cleanup.

competencies.statement: Test accepted, invalid, absent, unauthorized, forbidden, stale, duplicate, oversized, canceled, timed-out, dependency-failed, and changed-schema cases.
misconceptions: High line coverage proves the handler contract and error mapping are correct.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Test accepted, invalid, absent, unauthorized, forbidden, stale, duplicate, oversized, canceled, timed-out, dependency-failed, and changed-schema cases.

competencies.statement: Fuzz bounded request targets, headers, JSON, cursors, signatures, and policy inputs with invariants, seed corpus, reproducibility, and no live authority.
misconceptions: A fuzz test is useful only if it discovers a crash rather than a semantic invariant violation.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Fuzz bounded request targets, headers, JSON, cursors, signatures, and policy inputs with invariants, seed corpus, reproducibility, and no live authority.

competencies.statement: Run race detection, concurrency schedules, leak checks, load profiles, resource limits, and failure injection while separating correctness from capacity claims.
misconceptions: Passing go test -race proves production has no races, deadlocks, leaks, or overload failures.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Run race detection, concurrency schedules, leak checks, load profiles, resource limits, and failure injection while separating correctness from capacity claims.

competencies.statement: Emit one structured request completion record with request and trace IDs, method, route pattern, status, duration, bytes, trusted peer class, and redaction.
misconceptions: Logging the raw URL and all request headers is the most useful production diagnostic record.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Emit one structured request completion record with request and trace IDs, method, route pattern, status, duration, bytes, trusted peer class, and redaction.

competencies.statement: Measure duration distributions, active requests, request and response bytes, status classes, cancellations, rejects, saturation, and dependencies with bounded attributes.
misconceptions: Using resource ID as a metric label makes endpoint latency easier to diagnose safely.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Measure duration distributions, active requests, request and response bytes, status classes, cancellations, rejects, saturation, and dependencies with bounded attributes.

competencies.statement: Apply current OpenTelemetry server span kind, low-cardinality route, method, status, error.type, network and server attributes, propagation, sampling, and 4xx semantics.
misconceptions: Every HTTP 4xx response should set a server span status of Error.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Apply current OpenTelemetry server span kind, low-cardinality route, method, status, error.type, network and server attributes, propagation, sampling, and 4xx semantics.

competencies.statement: Separate liveness, readiness, startup, dependency, drain, and deep diagnostics; bound their cost; and prevent sensitive disclosure.
misconceptions: Liveness should fail whenever a downstream database is briefly unavailable so the process restarts.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Separate liveness, readiness, startup, dependency, drain, and deep diagnostics; bound their cost; and prevent sensitive disclosure.

competencies.statement: Use benchmarks, pprof in a protected plane, traces, saturation, tail latency, allocation, contention, load shape, and changed-fault evidence for capacity and incidents.
misconceptions: A lower microbenchmark nanoseconds-per-operation proves the deployed server can handle more user traffic.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Use benchmarks, pprof in a protected plane, traces, saturation, tail latency, allocation, contention, load shape, and changed-fault evidence for capacity and incidents.

competencies.statement: Describe OpenAPI 3.2 operations, servers, parameters, request bodies, responses, problems, schemas, security, callbacks, and webhooks with tool limits.
misconceptions: Generated OpenAPI is automatically complete and correct when every route appears once.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Describe OpenAPI 3.2 operations, servers, parameters, request bodies, responses, problems, schemas, security, callbacks, and webhooks with tool limits.

competencies.statement: Compare specification, route registration, transport types, validators, examples, tests, auth policy, and observed responses to detect bidirectional drift.
misconceptions: Schema validation alone proves handler semantics, authorization, and side effects match documentation.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Compare specification, route registration, transport types, validators, examples, tests, auth policy, and observed responses to detect bidirectional drift.

competencies.statement: Classify additive and breaking changes across fields, nullability, validation, status, errors, ordering, auth, caching, and behavior; then plan deprecation and migration.
misconceptions: Adding an optional response field is always non-breaking for every client and signature scheme.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Classify additive and breaking changes across fields, nullability, validation, status, errors, ordering, auth, caching, and behavior; then plan deprecation and migration.

competencies.statement: Build once, verify artifact and config, expand storage safely, stage readiness, canary, observe, drain with Shutdown, contract storage, and rollback by invariant.
misconceptions: If the new binary starts and health is green, schema contraction and full traffic are safe immediately.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Build once, verify artifact and config, expand storage safely, stage readiness, canary, observe, drain with Shutdown, contract storage, and rollback by invariant.

competencies.statement: Defend outcomes, semantics, routes, architecture, limits, persistence, identity, policy, browser and proxy trust, tests, observability, capacity, documentation, release, recovery, and residual risk.
misconceptions: A green deployment and passing smoke request complete production readiness and eliminate the need for rollback drills.
masteryEvidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Defend outcomes, semantics, routes, architecture, limits, persistence, identity, policy, browser and proxy trust, tests, observability, capacity, documentation, release, recovery, and residual risk.

credential.title: HTTP Servers in Go 1.26: Secure Production APIs Certificate

modules.title: Service Outcomes, Boundaries, and Server Lifecycle
modules.description: A civic incident API has endpoints but no stated user outcome, ownership boundary, startup contract, dependency readiness, shutdown policy, or evidence connecting a request to durable effect. Learners cumulatively produce service outcome, boundary, and lifecycle map through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define a server outcome with user, client, operator, security, and accessibility evidence instead of treating endpoint count as success.
Trace user agent, client, proxy, gateway, origin handler, dependency, and response roles without assigning one component evidence it cannot observe.
Distinguish process, listener, connection, stream, request, handler, goroutine, dependency, and durable-state lifetimes.

modules.title: HTTP Semantics, Resources, Methods, and Status Contracts
modules.description: A task service uses POST for every action, mutates state on GET, returns 200 for rejected work, and retries duplicate writes without a resource or method contract. Learners cumulatively produce resource, method, status, and retry semantics table through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Separate resource identity, current server state, selected representation, transfer content, and backing storage model.
Apply safe, idempotent, and cacheable method semantics independently and preserve them through handler behavior.
Choose status, Location, validators, and response content for create, replace, partial update, asynchronous work, and deletion.

modules.title: ServeMux Patterns, Precedence, Path Values, and Route Safety
modules.description: A Go 1.26 service mixes legacy prefix routes with method and wildcard patterns, conflicts at startup, misroutes escaped slashes, and assumes slash redirects still use a permanent status. Learners cumulatively produce validated ServeMux route and precedence table through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Author Go 1.26 ServeMux patterns with optional method, host, literal segments, single wildcards, remainder wildcards, and end markers.
Derive most-specific precedence as request-set inclusion and detect conflicting patterns before startup.
Use Request.PathValue while distinguishing segment unescaping, escaped slash data, raw query, cleaned paths, and invalid identifiers.

modules.title: Handlers, Middleware, Dependencies, and Application Architecture
modules.description: Handlers construct database pools and loggers per request, middleware changes response order, mutable globals leak across tests, and business rules cannot run without HTTP fixtures. Learners cumulatively produce composed handler and dependency boundary through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use http.Handler and HandlerFunc as request-to-response boundaries while keeping response ownership and handler lifetime explicit.
Compose recovery, request ID, trust, authentication, authorization, limit, observation, and application middleware in a defended order.
Construct long-lived immutable dependencies at startup and inject narrow interfaces without service locators or per-request pool creation.

modules.title: Request Targets, Host Authority, Client Identity, and Proxy Trust
modules.description: An origin accepts arbitrary Host values, constructs password-reset links from untrusted forwarded fields, rate-limits a proxy address, and logs raw targets containing secrets. Learners cumulatively produce request authority and trusted-proxy decision record through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Interpret Method, URL, Host, RequestURI, Header, Body, Trailer, RemoteAddr, TLS, Pattern, and Context using server-side semantics.
Validate authoritative host and port against a configured allowlist before routing, link construction, cache keys, or tenant selection.
Accept Forwarded or X-Forwarded values only from configured proxy hops and derive client, scheme, and host with a documented traversal rule.

modules.title: Header Fields, Representations, Negotiation, Validators, and Caching
modules.description: Clients send repeated and malformed media fields, the server sniffs content, omits Vary, caches personalized responses, and computes validators from unstable JSON ordering. Learners cumulatively produce representation selection and cache contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Handle case-insensitive field names, repeated values, list syntax, hop-by-hop fields, size limits, and canonical map behavior without naïve splitting.
Validate request Content-Type and parameters, select an acceptable response representation, and return 415 or 406 with a documented default.
Set Content-Type, Content-Length strategy, Location, validators, Cache-Control, Vary, security fields, and trailers before committing response headers.

modules.title: Bounded Bodies, JSON, Forms, Multipart Uploads, and Validation
modules.description: A create endpoint trusts Content-Length, decodes one JSON value but ignores trailing data, accepts unknown fields, buffers uploads in memory, and reports internal parser errors to clients. Learners cumulatively produce bounded request decoding and validation pipeline through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use MaxBytesReader and server limits to bound actual body reads independently of absent, false, compressed, or chunked Content-Length.
Decode exactly one JSON value, reject trailing content and unknown fields when the contract is closed, and distinguish syntax, type, and size failures.
Separate transport shape validation from domain invariants, normalize only defined values, accumulate safe field errors, and preserve causality internally.

modules.title: Responses, Problem Details, Streaming, and Protocol-Aware Output
modules.description: A service writes bodies before status and headers, exposes stack traces, treats cancellation as 500, flushes unbounded streams, and assumes optional ResponseWriter interfaces always exist. Learners cumulatively produce single-commit response and streaming contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Predict implicit 200, content sniffing, automatic length, one final status, informational responses, and header commitment order.
Serialize RFC 9457 problem type, title, status, detail, instance, extensions, and application/problem+json without leaking implementation data.
Map validation, authentication, authorization, absence, conflict, precondition, limit, timeout, cancellation, dependency, and internal failures consistently.

modules.title: Request Context, Deadlines, Cancellation, and Cleanup
modules.description: A request starts database and outbound work with background contexts, ignores disconnects, exhausts its entire deadline before cleanup, and leaks goroutines after middleware returns. Learners cumulatively produce deadline budget and cancellation propagation trace through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace when an incoming request context is canceled and pass it through every request-scoped operation without storing it in long-lived structs.
Allocate caller deadline across validation, database, outbound dependency, response, and cleanup reserves instead of stacking independent full timeouts.
Distinguish client cancellation, server deadline, dependency timeout, shutdown, overload, and internal failure for response and observability decisions.

modules.title: Server Configuration, HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, TLS, and Graceful Shutdown
modules.description: A public server uses package defaults, has zero timeouts, trusts unlimited headers, enables cleartext HTTP/2 accidentally, serves weak TLS, and terminates active requests on deploy. Learners cumulatively produce explicit http.Server protocol and shutdown configuration through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Configure ReadHeaderTimeout, body strategy, WriteTimeout, IdleTimeout, MaxHeaderBytes, and per-handler deadlines from threat and workload evidence.
Configure Server.Protocols for HTTP/1 and TLS HTTP/2, treat UnencryptedHTTP2 as explicit h2c authority, and name HTTP/3 as outside net/http server support.
Set HTTP2Config stream, frame, compression-table, flow-control, ping, write-byte, and error-count boundaries without confusing transport and server fields.

modules.title: Concurrency, Shared State, Backpressure, Rate Limits, and Load Shedding
modules.description: Concurrent handlers race on a map, launch unbounded goroutines, queue work after clients leave, share one global rate limit, and accept more expensive work than dependencies can sustain. Learners cumulatively produce concurrency budget and overload decision model through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Treat handlers as concurrent, protect shared state with ownership or synchronization, and verify with race tests under changed schedules.
Bound active expensive work with semaphores, worker pools, queue capacity, context-aware admission, fairness, and per-tenant partitions.
Choose trusted identity, token bucket or window semantics, burst, refill, distributed consistency, Retry-After, and bypass policy from abuse risk.

modules.title: SQL Repositories, Pools, Transactions, and Request Consistency
modules.description: Handlers open a new database handle per request, concatenate resource IDs, leak rows, perform multi-step writes outside transactions, and return before commit ambiguity is resolved. Learners cumulatively produce context-bound repository and transaction contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Treat sql.DB as a concurrent pool, open once, verify readiness, tune open and idle capacity and lifetime, observe Stats, and close at process shutdown.
Use dialect parameters, QueryContext, Scan, rows Close and Err, bounded result sets, and error identity without constructing SQL from input.
Define atomic invariants, BeginTx with context and isolation, defer rollback, commit exactly once, and handle PostgreSQL Read Committed changes.

modules.title: Resource APIs, Pagination, Idempotency, and Concurrency Control
modules.description: A list endpoint skips and duplicates rows during concurrent writes, create retries duplicate charges, PATCH silently overwrites fields, and response schemas expose storage columns. Learners cumulatively produce stable resource and mutation consistency contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define stable transport schemas, domain models, persistence records, optional and null semantics, field ownership, and evolution boundaries separately.
Build opaque cursor pagination from a unique stable order, bound page size, preserve filters and authorization, and test inserts and deletes between pages.
Scope and validate idempotency keys, bind request fingerprints, serialize first execution, persist terminal outcome, expire records, and reject mismatched replay.

modules.title: Authentication, Sessions, Passwords, Bearer Tokens, and Credential Recovery
modules.description: A service invents password hashing, trusts JWT payloads before verification, accepts any algorithm and audience, stores long-lived bearer tokens in logs, and leaves sessions valid after recovery. Learners cumulatively produce authentication and credential lifecycle policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Separate presented credential, verification, authenticated principal, session, assurance, freshness, and downstream authorization.
Use reviewed password hashing, bounded comparison, generic failures, rate and abuse controls, MFA or step-up policy, and secure recovery lifecycle.
Issue opaque sessions with rotation, server-side state or revocation, Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite, Path, Domain, expiry, fixation defense, and logout invalidation.

modules.title: Authorization, Object Ownership, Properties, Functions, and Tenancy
modules.description: Authenticated users can enumerate another tenant’s records, mass-assign privileged fields, call admin actions through hidden routes, and infer denied objects from response differences. Learners cumulatively produce deny-by-default authorization decision matrix through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define subject, action, resource, tenant, properties, relationship, context, policy version, and decision evidence for every protected operation.
Load or constrain the target within the authorized tenant and ownership relationship before read, write, delete, export, or nested access.
Allowlist readable and writable properties by operation and principal, reject server-owned fields, and prevent over-posting through explicit command types.

modules.title: Browser Origins, CORS, CSRF, Cookies, and Security Fields
modules.description: A browser API reflects arbitrary Origin with credentials, mutates state on GET, accepts ambient cookies cross-site, caches preflight incorrectly, and treats CORS as server authorization. Learners cumulatively produce browser request trust and cross-origin policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Distinguish origin, site, same-origin policy, CORS read permission, preflight, credentials, and server authorization.
Validate exact origins, methods, requested headers, credential mode, exposed headers, max age, Vary, null origin, and rejection behavior.
Use safe-method invariants, Go CrossOriginProtection, Sec-Fetch-Site and Origin policy, tokens when needed, and narrowly reviewed bypasses.

modules.title: Webhooks, Replay Defense, SSRF, Abuse, and Sensitive Business Flows
modules.description: A webhook parses JSON before verifying raw bytes, accepts replay forever, fetches callback URLs from users, retries non-idempotent work, and rate-limits requests but not expensive business actions. Learners cumulatively produce webhook authenticity, replay, and abuse defense through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Bound and retain exact raw body bytes, select expected signature scheme and key, compare in constant time, and only then parse and authorize the event.
Validate timestamp or nonce window, event ID uniqueness, tenant, type, ordering, duplicate delivery, and durable processing state before effects.
Acknowledge only after durable acceptance, move long work to bounded processing, classify retries, make effects idempotent, and expose operator recovery.

modules.title: Reverse Proxies, Forwarded Trust, Message Framing, and Smuggling Defense
modules.description: A gateway preserves client Forwarded fields, uses deprecated ReverseProxy.Director, disagrees with an upstream about malformed framing, forwards hop-by-hop fields, and constructs unsafe backend queries. Learners cumulatively produce proxy rewrite and parser-consistency threat model through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace downstream and upstream connections, hop-by-hop versus end-to-end fields, termination points, authority, scheme, client address, and failure ownership.
Use ReverseProxy.Rewrite with separate inbound and outbound requests, SetURL and reviewed SetXForwarded behavior, and reject deprecated Director trust assumptions.
Strip untrusted forwarding fields, rebuild them only at trusted hops, constrain backend targets, remove hop-by-hop state, and prevent header spoofing.

modules.title: Handler Tests, Live-Protocol Tests, Fuzzing, Race Detection, and Contracts
modules.description: Tests call handler functions directly, inspect ResponseRecorder fields incorrectly, omit middleware and cancellation, never use TLS or concurrency, and assert only one status code. Learners cumulatively produce layered deterministic server test system through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use httptest.NewRequestWithContext, NewRecorder, Result, headers, body limits, route values, and table cases while naming recorder limitations.
Use NewServer or NewTLSServer for routing, middleware, redirects, cookies, protocol, streaming, and client-server integration with deterministic cleanup.
Test accepted, invalid, absent, unauthorized, forbidden, stale, duplicate, oversized, canceled, timed-out, dependency-failed, and changed-schema cases.

modules.title: Structured Observability, Health, Profiling, Capacity, and Incidents
modules.description: Logs contain tokens and raw paths, metrics use user IDs, traces mark every 404 as a server error, readiness ignores database saturation, and a p50 benchmark drives release. Learners cumulatively produce low-cardinality service evidence and incident dashboard through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Emit one structured request completion record with request and trace IDs, method, route pattern, status, duration, bytes, trusted peer class, and redaction.
Measure duration distributions, active requests, request and response bytes, status classes, cancellations, rejects, saturation, and dependencies with bounded attributes.
Apply current OpenTelemetry server span kind, low-cardinality route, method, status, error.type, network and server attributes, propagation, sampling, and 4xx semantics.

modules.title: OpenAPI 3.2, Compatibility, Deployment, and Production Defense
modules.description: An undocumented API changes nullable fields, serves stale generated docs, deploys a new binary before migrations, drains no traffic, and cannot tie an incident response to source or schema. Learners cumulatively produce versioned API contract and production release defense through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Describe OpenAPI 3.2 operations, servers, parameters, request bodies, responses, problems, schemas, security, callbacks, and webhooks with tool limits.
Compare specification, route registration, transport types, validators, examples, tests, auth policy, and observed responses to detect bidirectional drift.
Classify additive and breaking changes across fields, nullability, validation, status, errors, ordering, auth, caching, and behavior; then plan deprecation and migration.
